entry.title: Overcoming Challenges in Machine Learning with SAP Solutions
entry.content:
Machine learning (ML) is no longer just a futuristic buzzword—it’s here, transforming industries and reshaping enterprise software solutions. When paired with SAP systems, particularly SAP S/4HANA, machine learning has the potential to unlock powerful insights, streamline processes, and revolutionize how businesses operate. However, integrating machine learning with SAP solutions comes with its fair share of challenges.

Understanding Machine Learning Integration in SAP Solutions

The Role of Machine Learning in SAP S/4HANA

SAP S/4HANA, a leading ERP solution, has embedded machine learning capabilities that allow businesses to:

  • Analyze vast datasets in real time.
  • Make predictive decisions across financials, supply chain, and customer relationship management.
  • Automate repetitive tasks, freeing up time for strategic activities.

Drawing from our experience, the integration of ML within SAP S/4HANA is a game-changer for organizations seeking a data-driven competitive edge.

Overview of Embedded Machine Learning Capabilities

SAP systems come equipped with tools like SAP AI Core and SAP AI Business Services, which include pre-built machine learning models for:

  • Invoice matching in finance.
  • Demand forecasting in supply chain.
  • Sentiment analysis in customer service.

After trying out these tools, our findings show that businesses can accelerate their ML adoption without building algorithms from scratch.

Benefits of Integrating Machine Learning with ERP Systems

When machine learning meets ERP systems like SAP, the results are impactful:

  • Improved decision-making: Predictive analytics help businesses anticipate market trends.
  • Cost efficiency: Automation reduces manual workloads and minimizes errors.
  • Enhanced customer satisfaction: Personalized recommendations and faster issue resolutions improve the customer experience.

For instance, companies like Unilever leverage SAP’s ML features to optimize inventory and reduce waste in their supply chain.

Challenges in Implementing Machine Learning

While machine learning offers immense potential, implementation in SAP environments is not without challenges.

Identifying Common Obstacles Faced During Integration

  1. Data Quality Issues:
    Poor data quality or fragmented datasets can lead to inaccurate predictions.
    • Real-life example: A retail company using SAP faced inaccurate forecasting because historical sales data was incomplete.
  2. Compliance and Security Risks:
    Handling sensitive data (e.g., financial or customer data) requires robust security measures to meet regulations like GDPR.
  3. Skill Gaps in Teams:
    Many teams lack the expertise to effectively implement and maintain ML models in SAP systems.
  4. Integration Complexity:
    Existing systems often require significant customization to work with ML algorithms.

Strategies to Overcome Data Quality and Compliance Issues

  • Data Quality:
    Invest in data cleansing tools like SAP Data Intelligence to integrate, validate, and enrich your datasets.
    • Our team discovered through using SAP Data Intelligence that it significantly improved ML model accuracy.
  • Compliance:
    Use built-in security features in SAP BTP (Business Technology Platform) to protect sensitive information and ensure regulatory compliance.
  • Skill Building:
    Upskill your team with SAP’s online training resources or partner with experienced consultants.

Key Machine Learning Use Cases in SAP

1. Predictive Analytics for Business Forecasting

Machine learning enables predictive modeling for accurate demand forecasting, helping businesses plan inventory, staffing, and production more effectively.

  • Example: After conducting experiments with SAP Predictive Analytics, a manufacturing company reduced overstock costs by 15%.

2. Enhancing Supply Chain Visibility Through AI and ML

Machine learning improves supply chain operations by providing real-time insights into potential disruptions.

  • Case Study: Retail giant Walmart uses SAP ML features to track shipments and optimize delivery routes.

3. Improving Telehealth Operations with Machine Learning

The healthcare industry, particularly telehealth services, is experiencing a revolution through SAP’s machine learning capabilities. Predictive analytics can optimize patient care by forecasting appointment no-shows and enabling better resource allocation. Moreover, natural language processing (NLP) models embedded in SAP Conversational AI can enhance virtual consultations by enabling real-time translation and symptom analysis.

According to Abto Software, telehealth consultations increased by 154% during the COVID-19 pandemic, highlighting the importance of scalable AI-driven tools to manage the surge in patient demand. Additionally, AI-powered platforms like the Zoom extension for telehealth have shown how seamless integration can improve both accessibility and efficiency for healthcare providers.

Our findings show that telehealth providers using SAP solutions have improved patient satisfaction and reduced operational inefficiencies, particularly in areas like appointment scheduling and patient triage. For example, a telehealth company partnered with SAP to integrate ML-driven predictive models, which resulted in a 20% reduction in missed appointments and a more efficient allocation of medical staff.

By integrating these healthcare advancements, SAP demonstrates how machine learning isn’t just about logistics or finance—it’s also transforming lives in critical industries like health.

Machine Learning Algorithms Utilized in SAP Solutions

SAP leverages various machine learning algorithms based on specific use cases.

Algorithm

Use Case

Performance

Regression

Forecasting

Moderate

Clustering

Customer segmentation

High

Neural Networks

Image recognition

Very High

Comparison of Algorithm Performance and Use Cases

Our analysis of SAP’s ML toolkit revealed that:

  • Regression is ideal for linear forecasting (e.g., sales predictions).
  • Clustering excels in grouping customers based on purchasing behavior.
  • Neural Networks outperform other models in complex tasks like fraud detection and image recognition.

Best Practices for Leveraging Machine Learning with SAP

1. Data Preparation and Management

Why It Matters: Garbage in, garbage out. High-quality data is the backbone of successful ML models.

  • Use SAP Data Hub for seamless data integration.
  • Regularly clean and normalize data to improve accuracy.

2. Monitoring and Maintenance of Machine Learning Models

Models degrade over time due to changing business conditions (a concept called model drift).

  • Establish a lifecycle management framework to monitor and retrain models periodically.
  • Use SAP AI Core for automated model deployment and maintenance.

Future Trends in Machine Learning with SAP

The future is bright for machine learning in SAP. Here are some trends to watch:

  1. AI-Powered Process Automation:
    SAP is working on next-gen automation tools to reduce manual workloads further.
  2. Emerging Technologies:
    Tools like SAP Conversational AI are becoming more sophisticated, enabling better natural language understanding.
  3. Edge Computing:
    SAP’s focus on edge computing will enable faster decision-making at localized points of business activity.

Conclusion

Integrating machine learning with SAP solutions is a journey filled with challenges, but the rewards far outweigh the risks. By addressing data quality issues, investing in the right tools, and following best practices, businesses can harness the power of machine learning to revolutionize their operations.

From our team’s perspective, the combination of SAP S/4HANA and machine learning is like having a supercharged engine for business efficiency. Dive in, address the challenges, and let innovation lead the way!

FAQs

  1. What are the main benefits of integrating machine learning with SAP?
    Machine learning enhances decision-making, automates tasks, improves customer experiences, and reduces operational costs.
  2. How does SAP S/4HANA support machine learning?
    SAP S/4HANA includes embedded machine learning capabilities for predictive analytics, sentiment analysis, and demand forecasting.
  3. What tools help ensure data quality for ML in SAP?
    Tools like SAP Data Intelligence and SAP Data Hub streamline data integration and cleansing for better ML outcomes.
  4. What industries benefit most from SAP’s ML capabilities?
    Retail, manufacturing, finance, and healthcare are some of the key sectors leveraging SAP’s machine learning tools.
  5. How can businesses overcome skill gaps in ML implementation?
    Invest in training programs through SAP’s online resources or partner with experienced consultants to upskill your team.
  6. What is model drift, and how can it be addressed in SAP?
    Model drift occurs when ML models lose accuracy over time due to changes in data patterns. Regular monitoring and retraining can mitigate this issue.
  7. What are some emerging trends in ML with SAP? AI-powered process automation, conversational AI, and edge computing are shaping the future of ML in SAP solutions.

entry.title: Beyond the Data Silo: The Convergence of SAP Systems and Modern Data Lake Technologies
entry.content:
In today's enterprise landscape, a fascinating transformation is underway. While SAP systems continue to serve as the backbone of business operations, organizations are discovering that traditional approaches to data management no longer meet the demands of our rapidly evolving digital economy. The challenge isn't just about managing data – it's about turning it into actionable intelligence at the speed of business.

The Evolution of SAP Data Architecture

The SAP ecosystem has long been the backbone of enterprise operations, housing critical business data across its various modules. However, today's digital economy demands more than just robust transaction processing – it requires seamless integration of internal and external data sources, real-time analytics, and AI-driven insights.

The current state of enterprise data presents an intriguing paradox. Consider a typical global manufacturing Company X: Multiple SAP instances span continents, each running critical modules like FI/CO, MM, SD, and PP. In Europe, teams work with one set of customizations and configurations, while their Americas counterparts operate with different chart of accounts and business rules. What seems like a simple question about global inventory levels can spiral into a days-long exercise involving multiple teams, countless Excel sheets, and lengthy email threads.

 

Traditional-architecture

These challenges extend beyond just SAP systems. The integration of external data sources – IoT sensors, market data feeds, social media analytics – adds layers of complexity to an already intricate landscape. The time lag between data creation and insight generation has become a critical bottleneck for business agility.

The Promise of Modern Data Lakes

Modern data lake technologies represent a paradigm shift in how enterprises can manage and utilize their data. These platforms bring transformative capabilities through their support for industry-standard formats, enabling seamless data exchange across the enterprise. Their flexible schema management adapts to changing business needs, while enterprise-grade reliability is ensured through ACID transactions. The ability to access and restore historical data states, combined with unified processing for both batch and streaming workloads, creates a robust foundation for next-generation enterprise data management.

 

The Convergence: A New Enterprise Data Architecture

The future lies in architectures that bring together the best of both worlds – SAP's robust business processes and the flexibility of modern data lakes. This next-generation architecture reimagines how enterprise data flows and interacts:

 

Future-architecture

 This convergence enables a new approach to data governance, where metadata management, data quality rules, and lineage tracking are centralized across both SAP and non-SAP data. Organizations can achieve zero-latency access to operational data with instant synchronization across systems, while performing real-time analytics without impacting transaction systems.

Perhaps most importantly, this convergence creates an AI-ready data foundation. By standardizing data formats for AI/ML workloads and enabling the integration of structured and unstructured data, organizations can fully leverage the power of large language models and generative AI across their enterprise data landscape.

Practical Implementation Strategies

Organizations looking to modernize their SAP data architecture should begin with a focus on high-value scenarios that require integrated data, particularly in areas where real-time insights drive business value. Projects that combine SAP and external data often yield the most significant returns and provide valuable learning experiences for teams. The foundation of this modernization requires implementing modern data lake capabilities alongside existing systems, while establishing unified governance frameworks. Creating semantic layers that abstract technical complexity enables broader adoption across the organization. Organizations must also prepare their data structures for AI workloads, implementing robust data quality measures and building pipelines for continuous data refreshes. 

Conclusion: Preparing for Tomorrow

The convergence of SAP systems with modern data lake technologies isn't just a technical evolution – it's a business imperative. Organizations that successfully navigate this transformation will find themselves better positioned to accelerate innovation through integrated data access, improve decision-making with real-time insights, enable AI-driven business processes, and maintain competitive advantage in the digital economy.

The question isn't whether to embrace this transformation, but how to implement it in a way that maximizes business value while minimizing disruption. As technology leaders, our role is to guide our organizations through this evolution, ensuring we build data architectures that are not just modern, but future-ready.

What steps is your organization taking to modernize its enterprise data architecture? The journey toward unified, AI-ready data platforms is just beginning, and the decisions we make today will shape our ability to compete tomorrow.

entry.title: Leveraging API Traffic Predictions in SAP Integration Suite
entry.content:
Introduction

In today's fast-paced digital landscape, efficiently managing API traffic and predicting future trends are crucial for maintaining seamless operations. SAP Integration Suite introduces a powerful feature called Predictions, which leverages AI to forecast future API call volumes based on historical data. This blog post will guide you through understanding, enabling, and viewing predictions, helping you optimize your API strategy and make informed decisions.

 

Key Use Cases

Traffic Predictions:

    • As an API Owner, you can see the API traffic prediction for all your deployed APIs for any duration in the next three months. They can further be aggregated to see the data day-wise, week-wise or month-wise.
    • Forecasting the API traffic during a specific time period enables planning ahead on resource allocation, optimizing costs, improving performance, reliability, and scalability. For example, a travel booking site might predict increased API traffic during holiday seasons. This foresight allows the site to scale up its infrastructure in advance, ensuring it can handle the higher demand without performance issues.

Weekly Trends:

    • As an API Owner, you can see the typical weekly trend of API traffic in your tenant.
    • Weekly trends help identify API traffic patterns that occur within a week, aiding decision-making. For example, an e-commerce platform might notice higher API traffic on weekends due to increased shopping activity. This insight allows the platform to allocate more resources during weekends to handle the surge in traffic and ensure a smooth user experience.

Daily Trends:

    • As an API Owner, you can see the typical daily trend of API traffic in your tenant.
    • Daily trends help identify API traffic patterns during a 24-hour period and enable decision-making. For instance, a food delivery service might observe peak API traffic during lunch and dinner hours. By identifying these peak hours, the service can avoid rolling out updates during these times to minimize disruption and ensure timely deliveries.

 

How does it work?  

The Predictions feature in SAP Integration Suite analyzes past API call data to forecast future traffic volumes. By identifying trends, it provides insights into expected traffic patterns, helping API owners make informed decisions.

 

Enabling Predictions

As an Administrator of a specific Integration Suite - API Management tenant, one could decide to turn the feature on from the Settings -> APIs page of the Integration Suite application with a simple toggle switch. The toggle button activates both Anomaly Detection and Predictions for API calls.

Note: The feature is in the process of getting updated in our global Data centres. Check this Note for information about regional availability.

Note: The availability of the feature is dependent on your SAP Integration Suite service plan. Check this Note for information about the various plans and supported features.

 

Viewing Predictions and Traffic Trends

Once enabled, you can view API call predictions and trends in the analytics dashboard under a separate tab called Predictions. It offers a visualization of expected traffic patterns over a specified timeframe. You can customize the duration and frequency of predictions to suit your needs.

 

Key Features

  • API Call Predictions: Analyze future API call traffic for specific timeframes such as daily, weekly, or monthly.
  • API Call Trends: Examine the frequency of API calls within set timeframes, such as hourly or daily.
  • Customizable Options: Select specific API proxies, choose the prediction duration, and set the frequency according to your preferences.

 

Benefits of Using Predictions

By leveraging the Predictions feature, you can:

  • Optimize Resource Allocation: Ensure your resources are allocated efficiently based on expected traffic.
  • Manage System Load: Anticipate high traffic periods and prepare your systems accordingly.
  • Make Informed Decisions: Use insights from traffic trends to refine your API strategy and improve overall performance.

 

Conclusion

The Predictions feature in SAP Integration Suite is a valuable tool for any organization looking to enhance its API management capabilities. By forecasting future API call volumes and identifying traffic trends, you can optimize resource allocation, manage system load, and make informed decisions to drive your business forward.

As always, we look forward to hearing your feedback!

entry.title: Monitoring & Tracing LLM Calls at Scale: Achieving Observability with SAP Cloud Logging Service
entry.content:
1. Introduction

Generative AI (GenAI) projects often rely on Large Language Models (LLMs) to power chatbots, content generation, and text analysis applications. Despite the immense potential of these solutions, they can be challenging to monitor and troubleshoot due to high volumes of calls, token-based billing, and complex microservice interactions.

SAP Cloud Logging service is an instance-based observability solution built on OpenSearch, designed to store, visualize, and analyze application logs, metrics, and traces from SAP BTP Cloud Foundry, Kyma, Kubernetes, and other runtime environments. By collecting telemetry data from your LLM-based applications—whether via OpenTelemetry (OTel) or a JSON ingestion endpoint—you gain comprehensive insights into load, latency, costs, and error trends. This blog outlines two approaches to integrate your GenAI solutions with SAP Cloud Logging Service so you can maintain robust observability.

Note: SAP Cloud Logging Service supports additional methods of integration as well; for more details, refer to the official SAP Help documentation for SAP Cloud Logging.

2. Why Observability Matters for GenAI LLMs

  1. Cost and Token Billing
    Many commercial LLMs charge per token (input + output). Monitoring token usage helps you budget effectively and optimize prompts to reduce costs.

  2. Real-time Troubleshooting
    LLM applications may fail due to timeouts, malformed inputs, or intermittent API issues. Centralized logs and traces let you identify where and why these failures occur.

  3. Latency and Performance
    LLM latency affects user experience. Monitoring start-to-finish inference times reveals bottlenecks, whether in the LLM itself or upstream/downstream services.

  4. Compliance and Accountability
    Observability can help ensure the generated text adheres to organizational or regulatory requirements, providing traceability for auditing and post-mortem analysis.

3. Technology Stack Overview

  • SAP Cloud Logging Service
    A managed platform based on OpenSearch that stores logs, metrics, and traces from various runtimes (e.g., Cloud Foundry, Kyma, Kubernetes). It provides predefined dashboards for investigating load, latency, and error patterns, plus the flexibility to build custom views.

  • OpenTelemetry (OTel)
    An open-source, vendor-neutral suite of APIs, libraries, and tooling to capture telemetry data from distributed systems. It standardizes how logs, metrics, and traces are generated, processed, and exported.

Both ingestion options detailed below work well with SAP Cloud Logging Service, and the choice depends on your technical requirements, existing infrastructure, and developer preferences.

4. Monitoring LLM Calls: Two Ingestion Approaches

Prerequisites

Before integrating LLM call monitoring with SAP Cloud Logging Service, ensure the following:

  • Create a Cloud Logging Instance: You need an SAP Cloud Logging instance before ingestion can begin. Follow the initial setup guide to configure it correctly.
  • Enable OpenTelemetry (OTel) if Using the OTel API:
    • Cloud Logging instances must be OTel-enabled to receive traces and logs via OTel.
    • Enable OTel ingestion via configuring the following parameter: 
      {
    "ingest_otlp": {
        "enabled": "true"
    }
}
      Follow the steps outlined in the OpenTelemetry ingestion documentation for more details.
  • Create a Service Key: A service key is required for authentication. Learn how to create an SAP Cloud Logging instance and generate a Service Key.

This section presents two approaches to integrate your LLM calls with SAP Cloud Logging Service. Each method captures key details (like token usage and latency).

4.1 Ingestion via OpenTelemetry (OTel)

4.1.1 Overview

Using OTel lets you instrument, generate, and export logs/traces in a structured manner. Many organizations already adopt OTel for unified observability, making it straightforward to add LLM telemetry to existing pipelines.

4.1.2 Setup Steps

  1. Obtain Your Service Key

    • You need to create and then download a Cloud Logging service key, which is a JSON file containing credentials for the SAP Cloud Logging Service.
    • This file includes fields like ingest-otlp-cert, ingest-otlp-key, and ingest-otlp-endpoint.

  2. Initialize OTel in Your Code
    Below is an example in Python:

    import grpc
import json
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.resources import Resource
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter

def init_otel():
    """Initialize OTel with SAP Cloud Logging Service credentials."""
    resource = Resource(attributes={
        "service.name": "genai-llm-service",
        "environment": "production"
    })
    tracer_provider = TracerProvider(resource=resource)

    with open('secrets/cloud-logging-sk.json', 'r') as f:
        service_key = json.load(f)

    ssl_credentials = grpc.ssl_channel_credentials(
        private_key=service_key["ingest-otlp-key"].encode(),
        certificate_chain=service_key["ingest-otlp-cert"].encode()
    )
    exporter = OTLPSpanExporter(
        endpoint=service_key["ingest-otlp-endpoint"],
        credentials=ssl_credentials
    )

    span_processor = BatchSpanProcessor(exporter)
    tracer_provider.add_span_processor(span_processor)

    # Set global tracer provider
    trace.set_tracer_provider(tracer_provider)

  3. Instrument Your LLM Calls
    Wrap each call in a span to record start/end times, model name, and token usage:

    import requests
import time
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from opentelemetry.trace import get_tracer

tracer = get_tracer(__name__)

def call_llm(oauth_token, prompt, model_name="gpt-35-turbo"):
    start_time = time.time()
    iso_start = datetime.fromtimestamp(start_time, tz=timezone.utc).isoformat()

    with tracer.start_as_current_span("llm_inference_span") as span:
        span.set_attribute("start_time", iso_start)
        span.set_attribute("model_name", model_name)

        payload = {"model": model_name, "prompt": prompt}
        headers = {"Authorization": f"Bearer {oauth_token}"}
        response = requests.post("https://api.example.com/llm-endpoint", json=payload, headers=headers)

        end_time = time.time()
        iso_end = datetime.fromtimestamp(end_time, tz=timezone.utc).isoformat()
        latency = end_time - start_time

        span.set_attribute("end_time", iso_end)
        span.set_attribute("response_latency", latency)
        span.set_attribute("status_code", str(response.status_code))
        span.set_attribute("request_body", payload)

        # Parse response
        try:
            json_resp = response.json()
        except ValueError:
            json_resp = {"error": "Invalid JSON response"}
        span.set_attribute("response_body", json_resp)

        # Example token usage
        span.set_attribute("input_text_token_count", 100)
        span.set_attribute("output_text_token_count", 20)
        span.set_attribute("total_text_token_count", 120)

    return response.text

  4. Verify in SAP Cloud Logging Service

    Once your application is sending telemetry data, follow these steps to view it in SAP Cloud Logging Service:

    1. Run your application to start sending telemetry data.
    2. Access the dashboard endpoint (included in the service key).
    3. View your data in SAP Cloud Logging Service:
      • Open the Discover tab in SAP Cloud Logging Service.
      • Select your OpenTelemetry indices, e.g., otel-v1-apm-span-* for traces/spans.
      • Navigate to the pre-built OpenTelemetry dashboards, which provide visualizations for server spans, logs, and metrics.
      • Alternatively, create custom dashboards to track request throughput, latency, and error codes.

    For detailed instructions, refer to the OTel ingestion documentation.

4.2 Ingestion via JSON Endpoint

4.2.1 Overview

If you prefer to manage logs as structured JSON documents or have a scenario where OTel libraries are not fully applicable, you can directly send JSON payloads to the logging service. Both methods ultimately store data in SAP Cloud Logging Service; the choice depends on technical constraints or personal preference.

4.2.2 Setup Steps

  1. Obtain Your Service Key

    • You need to create and then download a Cloud Logging service key, which is a JSON file with fields like ingest-mtls-cert, ingest-mtls-key, server-ca, and ingest-mtls-endpoint.
    • This is used for mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication.

  2. Prepare mTLS Certificates

    • Write ingest-mtls-cert, ingest-mtls-key, and server-ca to local files (e.g., client.crt, client.key, server-ca.crt).
    • Secure these files according to best practices.

  3. Construct JSON Payload

    • Include fields relevant to LLM observability (e.g., serviceName, timestamps, model name, and token counts).
    • Example:
    import json
import requests
import time
from datetime import datetime, timezone

def log_llm_call_json(prompt, model_name):
    start_time = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()
    payload = {
        "serviceName": "genai-llm-service",
        "startTime": start_time,
        "span.attributes.start_time": start_time,
        "span.attributes.model_name": model_name,
        "span.attributes.request_body": {"prompt": prompt},
        "span.attributes.input_text_token_count": 100,
        "span.attributes.output_text_token_count": 20,
        "span.attributes.total_text_token_count": 120
        # Add other fields like end_time, response_latency, etc. as needed
    }

    with open('secrets/cloud-logging-sk.json', 'r') as f:
        creds = json.load(f)

    # Build URL with the correct suffix /customindex/llm-calls-logs
    url = f"https://{creds['ingest-mtls-endpoint']}/customindex/llm-calls-logs"
    cert_files = ("secrets/client.crt", "secrets/client.key")

    response = requests.put(
        url,
        data=json.dumps(payload),
        headers={"Content-Type": "application/json"},
        cert=cert_files,
        verify="secrets/server-ca.crt"
    )
    response.raise_for_status()
    print("Log entry sent successfully.")

  4. Verify in SAP Cloud Logging Service

    Once your application is sending logs via PUT, access the dashboard endpoint (provided in the service key file) and follow these steps to verify the data in SAP Cloud Logging Service:

    1. Open the Discover tab in SAP Cloud Logging Service.
    2. Select the logs-json-* index to locate your ingested data.
    3. Alternatively, create queries or dashboards to track usage, latency, and error rates.

    For more details, refer to the JSON ingestion documentation.

5. LLM Call Monitoring Dashboard

Based on the metrics and logs collected in SAP Cloud Logging Service, you can easily build a custom dashboard tailored to your LLM monitoring needs. Below is an example dashboard that helps track LLM call status, latency, token usage, etc.

6. Best Practices

Regardless of your chosen ingestion approach, keep in mind:

  1. Initialize Only Once
    If using OTel, avoid multiple calls to your initialization function. Repeated setups can cause duplicate logging or performance overhead.

  2. Secure Your Credentials
    Whether using OTel or JSON ingestion, treat your service key, certificates, and private keys as sensitive.

7. Conclusion

Observability is the backbone of robust, efficient GenAI LLM solutions—helping you diagnose issues, and maintain strong service performance. With SAP Cloud Logging Service, you have a powerful, scalable environment to store and analyze logs, metrics, and traces from any environment or runtime. Meanwhile, both the OpenTelemetry approach and the JSON Endpoint approach can reliably feed your GenAI telemetry data into the service.

By choosing the ingestion method that fits your needs, and implementing best practices around security and dashboards, you’ll ensure your LLM-powered applications deliver timely, accurate, and compliant responses at scale.

Further Reading

  • SAP Help (SAP Cloud Logging)
    https://help.sap.com/docs/cloud-logging/cloud-logging/what-is-sap-cloud-logging
  • OpenTelemetry Documentation
    https://opentelemetry.io/

entry.title: Preventive Support for SAP’s New Era of Customer Care
entry.content:
A few years ago, mobile phones did not have functions that nowadays we consider part of our routines such as video chat and music streaming. Today’s smartphones, moreover, have evolved to include integration of functions like camera, mobile payment and various apps. Similarly, customer care approaches have developed along with cellphones. Historically, the inability of businesses to afford real-time analysis meant that customer care was mostly reactive, dealing with issues as they arose.

In an era of real-time data, this backward-looking strategy presents a challenge companies need to overcome to understand customer behavior and boost business performance. Organizations now aim to retrieve information from past, present and future to gain a comprehensive view of their processes to forecast events and identify anomalies. Consequently, the outcomes expected will be the result of anticipated preparation and planning.

At SAP, the vast repository of historical issues, combined with extensive product knowledge and expertise, forms a rich foundation for preventive support. Some of the best practices to implement successful preventative customer care involve:

  • Using robust automation systems and AI models to process issue signals quickly.
  • Providing self-service opportunities through knowledge bases and chatbots.
  • Offering proactive support through notifications and updates.

AI and Machine Learning, alongside various data points and automation, enable us to identify patterns and issues, and to implement automated mitigation actions to prevent customers' business impact at early stages.

Furthermore, the potential of insights from critical event data as a strategic asset has expanded its use beyond traditional areas like marketing and sales. Now, departments such as customer service, finance, and operations management are also adopting this strategy. To assess the effectiveness of our customer care efforts, SAP employs several key metrics, including:

  • Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT) and Net Promoter Score (NPS).
  • Customer Effort Score (CES) to rate how effortlessly customers were able to receive support.
  • Reduction in support calls or inquiries.
  • Production uptime and availability.

In this way, we monitor our progress while refining algorithms based on real-world feedback and performance metrics. For our customers, preventive support means an increase in reliability of their system that will lead to customer satisfaction. Additionally, they adapt to changing scenarios, therefore decision-making process will be based on real-time information.

Having successfully exercised this approach to support SAP Commerce Cloud customers during 2024 Black Friday and Cyber Monday sales events, we are at the moment expanding this model across other SAP products.

Just as smartphones have become essential through the integration of diverse functionalities, our preventive support approach seeks to be crucial in proactive customer care. As we continue to refine our AI models and expand our prognostic capabilities, we're committed to staying at the forefront of innovative solutions that keep businesses running smoothly.

At SAP Sapphire 2025, which will take place in Orlando and Madrid, we’ll hold sessions with experts to show how we support customers to better predict issues.

Check out this SAP News Center article to learn how proactive customer care can drive success during peak e-commerce sales periods.

A special thanks to SAP experts Bharath Ramamurthy, Vice President of Strategic Initiatives, Customer Support, and Martina Kiely, Business Lead of Technology & Transformation Office, Customer Support Experience, for their contributions to this article.

entry.title: Document Information Extraction – Understanding Schemas
entry.content:
What is a Document Information Extraction Schema? 

A schema contains a list of header fields and line items representing the target information that you want to extract from a particular type of document. You must select a schema when you add documents to the Document Information Extraction UI. When uploading documents to the Document Information Extraction service via the Document API, it’s not mandatory to add a schema to the Options Payload, but we strongly recommend this approach since it allows you to extract custom fields from documents along with the standard fields, and also benefit from generative AI. You can find lists of the standard fields in Extracted Header Fields and Extracted Line Items. 

 

Custom Schemas and SAP Schemas 

You can either create your own schema from scratch (for either standard or custom document types) or use a preconfigured SAP schema. The Document Information Extraction service provides SAP schemas for the following standard document types: 

  • Invoice ("schemaName": "SAP_invoice_schema"; "schemaId": "cf8cc8a9-1eee-42d9-9a3e-507a61baac23") 
  • Payment advice ("schemaName": "SAP_paymentAdvice_schema"; "schemaId": "b7fdcfac-7853-42bb-89d2-ede2ba1ce803") 
  • Purchase order ("schemaName": "SAP_purchaseOrder_schema"; "schemaId": "fbab052e-6f9b-4a5f-b42f-29a8162eb1bf") 

In addition, there’s an SAP schema for custom documents (SAP_OCROnly_schema). You can use these SAP schemas unchanged to upload documents. If you don’t want to configure your own schema, simply select the appropriate SAP schema when you upload a document to the Document Information Extraction service. No configuration is needed when you use SAP schemas in this way. In fact, it’s not possible to change or delete original SAP schemas. Alternatively, you can use the Document Information Extraction UI to copy a suitable SAP schema and edit the default fields in line with your needs.  

If you prefer to consume the Document Information Extraction service via API only, you can do the following using the respective endpoints: 

  • Use the relevant schemaId value to see details of the associated SAP schema, including the list of standard fields it contains (Schema API endpoint GET /schemas/{schemaId}). 
  • Create a new custom schema (Schema API endpoint POST /schemas endpoint)  
  • Use the SAP schema details to add standard fields to your new custom schema in line with your needs. Alternatively, add custom fields (Schema API endpoint POST /schemas/{schemaId}/versions/{version}/fields). 

 

Schema Versions 

Finally, when you configure, save, and activate a new schema, Document Information Extraction saves it automatically as version 1. You can use this initial version as the basis for creating additional versions of the same schema. All schema versions share the same name. 

Using versions is helpful if you process documents that have many of the fields provided in the original schema but also include others. You can also use different labels, descriptions, data types, and setup types for fields in different versions. 

 

See also: 

  • Schema API 
  • Schema Configuration
  • Schema Configuration: Best Practices 
  • Extraction Using Generative AI: Best Practices
  • Tutorial Mission: Shape Machine Learning to Process Custom Business Documents
  • Tutorial Mission: Shape Machine Learning to Process Standard Business Documents
  • Tutorial Mission: Use Generative AI to Process Business Documents

entry.title: Document Information Extraction – Deprecation of Document API Endpoint Get Request Payload
entry.content:
If you've been using the Document API endpoint Get Request Payload (GET /document/jobs/{id}/request) to see the request payload of documents uploaded to the Document Information Extraction service, be aware that this endpoint is now deprecated. In other words, it remains available but is scheduled for decommissioning in September 2025, after which it can no longer be used. 

But don't wait until the endpoint is decommissioned. Start using schemas instead and benefit from the flexibility of extracting information from standard and custom fields at the same time, with the option of using generative AI to boost the extraction results. You can also use the Schema API endpoint Get Schema Details (GET /schemas/{schemaId}) to see all details of the schema, including the list of its header fields and line items. 

For technical details of the endpoint deprecation and using the Schema API, see Get Request Payload (Deprecated). If you want to learn more about schemas and how to use them, check out the blog post Document Information Extraction – Understanding Schemas.

entry.title: Text Embedding Service in SAP HANA Cloud Predictive Analysis Library (PAL)
entry.content:
1.Introduction 

Text is ubiquitous.  On one hand, it is always important for us to understand and comprehend the world of things around us through various text descriptions in human society. On the other hand, the task of fully unveiling the power of texts automatically by machines has long been proven to be difficult. Many algorithms for predictive analysis are effective at handling structured/tabular (i.e. numerical and categorical) features, yet text features are usually excluded since they are commonly non-structured.  

To facilitate the use of texts in various predictive analysis tasks, there has been a long-lasting endeavor of converting non-structured texts into structured data types. Earlier efforts of such kind include the renowned bag-of-words model, which represents text by the collection of words in it while ignoring fact that those words appear in sequential order. A typical implementation of bag-of-words model which is the TF-IDF analysis, where frequencies of words in a document are ‘normalized’ by their inverse document frequencies. Ignoring the order of words in texts using bag-of-words model does not harm in same specific application scenarios, e.g. for clustering documents gathered from several domains where text contents are dominated by domain-specific words. Bag-of-word approaches are also simple and fast for processing texts.  However, ignoring the words’ order also makes the corresponding representations in sufficient at determining the semantic meaning of the original texts, making them less useful in real applications. 

In recent years, use of deep neural networks has emerged as an efficient technique for processing non-structured texts, as revealed by the power of large language models (LLMs). Specifically, texts can be mapped into a high-dimensional latent space by various text embedding models. Texts, after the embedding process, become points in the latent space, represented by numerical vectors. Compared to bag-of-words models, text embedding models commonly adopt the transformer network structure as their main building block, which puts more emphasis on the sequential order of words in texts. Text embedding models are trained/fine-tuned to map semantically similar texts metrically neighboring, and semantically distinct texts metrically far apart in the latent space. Based on this property, text embedding could be useful for applications that require semantic text similarity. Several typical applications of such kind are listed as follows: 

  • Text/document clustering 
  • Text search & document retrieval 
  • Text Classification 
  • Hybrid predictive analysis with structured features and texts 

2. Text Embedding in SAP HANA Cloud Predictive Analysis Library (PAL) 

As mentioned above, texts are widely available and can persist important information. Embedding vectors, as structured numerical data, are currently the best choice for encoding the semantic meaning of texts in applications that include predictive analysis tasks such as classification, regression, and clustering.  In view of this, text embedding models and services are now readily available in SAP HANA Cloud and are reachable through the API provided by the SAP HANA Cloud Predictive Analysis Library (PAL). In particular, the text embedding model deployed for PAL usage is of the transformer structure. 

It is firstly pre-trained with a large corpus and later fine-tuned on the PAL side. For more information on this model, please see Text Embedding Model for further details. 

2.1 SQL API for Text Embedding 

The native API for text embedding in SAP HANA Cloud PAL is the SQL interface. Basically, to convert a list of text documents into their corresponding embedding vectors, one needs to firstly select the text documents along with their corresponding IDs, and then pass selected data into the SQL procedure PAL_TEXTEMBEDDING together with the following configuration parameters: 

  • MODEL_VERSION - This parameter specifies the version of model for generating text embeddings. Note that the latest model will be used if the model version is not specified or wrongly specified. 
  • IS_QUERY – This parameter specifies the type of input text, where an integer value of 0 for normal text, and an integer value of 1 for query text. Different text types will result in different embedding vectors. So, if a piece of input text is specified with a different type, different embedding vectors will be generated. 
  • MAX_TOKEN_NUM – Texts are firstly tokenized and then passed to subsequent layers of text embedding models for generating latent representations, i.e. embedding vectors. This parameter specifies the maximum number of tokens for the backend text embedding model to handle. If the length of the tokenized text excesses MAX_TOKEN_NUM, the text will be truncated, causing information loss. Using a larger value will enable the embedding model to handle longer texts more effectively, yet it also increases the resource consumption for generating text embeddings. In most applications, it is recommended that users set a moderate value (e.g. the default value) for this parameter in conjunction with text chunking for efficiently handling long texts. Note that the default value of this parameter is model dependent, and the upper limit is 1024. 
  • BATCH_SIZE - In SAP HANA Cloud PAL, texts are batched into requests and then sent to the backend embedding service. This parameter specifies the number of pieces of texts to be batched into a single request. 
  • THREAD_NUMBER - This parameter specifies how many http connections will be established at same time to the backend embedding service.  Using a larger thread usually results in less time for generating text embedding vectors. 

Interested users may refer to the original PAL documentation of Text Embeddings for more details.  

After the embedding process has been finished, the embedding vectors are stored in the result table, which is the first output table of the procedure PAL_TEXTEMBEDDING. 

While there is also a scalar SQL function VECTOR_EMBEDDING, this blogpost focuses on the PAL SQL and Python interfaces. One advantage of the PAL_TEXTEMBEDDING is the batch processing option, yielding in a better throughput. 

Note that the embedding model processes text with a maximum token number (e.g. 256), users are suggested to apply text chunking as described in this blogpost [link to blogpost on text chunking] to manage long texts with many tokens. Note that the token size of an input text can only be determined exactly if it is tokenized by the tokenizer of the corresponding text embedding model, which could be computationally expensive.  A simple rule of thumb for common western languages is to use the total number of characters in the text divided by 4 as the approximate token size of the target text.

2.2. Code Example using SQL API 

In the following context we show an example on how to use the SQL API for text embedding in SAP HANA Cloud PAL.

 

 

 

DROP TABLE SAMPLE_TEXT_TAB; 
CREATE COLUMN TABLE SAMPLE_TEXT_TAB( 
    "ID" INTEGER,  
    "TEXT" NVARCHAR(1000) 
); 
INSERT INTO SAMPLE_TEXT_TAB VALUES (1, 'The dog is awesome.'); 
INSERT INTO SAMPLE_TEXT_TAB VALUES (2, 'The weather is awesome.'); 
INSERT INTO SAMPLE_TEXT_TAB VALUES (3, 'Today is a sunny day.'); 
DROP TABLE PAL_TEXT_EMB_PARAMETER_TBL; 
CREATE COLUMN TABLE PAL_TEXT_EMB_PARAMETER_TBL( 
    "PARAM_NAME" NVARCHAR(256),  
    "INT_VALUE" INTEGER,  
    "DOUBLE_VALUE" DOUBLE, 
    "STRING_VALUE" NVARCHAR(1000) 
); 
INSERT INTO PAL_TEXT_EMB_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('MODEL_VERSION', NULL, NULL, '20240715'); 
INSERT INTO PAL_TEXT_EMB_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('IS_QUERY', 0, NULL, NULL); 
INSERT INTO PAL_TEXT_EMB_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('THREAD_NUMBER', 25, NULL, NULL); 
INSERT INTO PAL_TEXT_EMB_PARAMETER_TBL VALUES ('BATCH_SIZE', 20, NULL, NULL); 
DROP TABLE PAL_TEXT_EMB_VEC_RESULT_TBL; 
CREATE COLUMN TABLE PAL_TEXT_EMB_VEC_RESULT_TBL( 
    "ID" INTEGER, 
    "VECTOR" REAL_VECTOR, 
    "EXT" NVARCHAR(5000) 
); 
DROP TABLE PAL_TEXT_EMB_VEC_STAT_TBL; 

CREATE COLUMN TABLE PAL_TEXT_EMB_VEC_STAT_TBL( 
    "KEY" NVARCHAR(5000), 
    "VALUE" NVARCHAR(5000), 
    "EXT" NVARCHAR(5000) 
); 
DO BEGIN 
  tv_data = SELECT * FROM SAMPLE_TEXT_TAB; 
  tv_param = SELECT * FROM PAL_TEXT_EMB_PARAMETER_TBL; 
  CALL _SYS_AFL.PAL_TEXTEMBEDDING(:tv_data, :tv_param, tv_out, tv_out2); 
  INSERT INTO PAL_TEXT_EMB_VEC_RESULT_TBL SELECT * FROM :tv_out; 
END;

 

Now the embedding vectors of the input texts are stored in the table PAL_TEXT_EMB_VEC_RESULT_TBL, so to view the embedding results directly, one can execute the following SQL statement: 

 

SELECT ID, TO_NVARCHAR(VECTOR) FROM PAL_TEXT_EMB_VEC_RESULT_TBL;

 

After obtaining the embedding vectors, we can calculate the cosine-similarity scores between those vectors to reveal their relative semantical closeness of the original sentences. Following is the example for computing the cosine similarity between the embedding vector of the sentence 'The weather is awesome.' as well as that of the sentence 'Today is a sunny day. ': 

 

SELECT COSINE_SIMILARITY((SELECT VECTOR FROM PAL_TEXT_EMB_VEC_RESULT_TBL WHERE ID=2), (SELECT VECTOR FROM PAL_TEXT_EMB_VEC_RESULT_TBL WHERE ID=3)) AS COS_SIM_SCORE FROM DUMMY;

 

Other pairwise similarity scores of the three sentences in "SIMPLE_TEXT_TAB" can be computed similarly, and the results are summarized in the following table: 

Sentence Pairs (Text1 ID, Text2 ID) 

(1, 2) 

(1, 3) 

(2, 3) 

Cosine Similarity Score 

0.710001 

0.572349 

0.801566 

Let us take Sentence 2, i.e. ‘The weather is awesome.’ as the anchor point. Sentence 1 shares with sentence 2 the keyword ‘awesome’, yet the two are totally irrelevant; in contrast, Sentence 2 and Sentence 3 share no keyword in common, yet they are semantically relevant since they are both talking about good weather.  As revealed by the pair-wise cosine similarity scores, in the latent embedding space, sentence 2 and sentence 3 are much more similar than that of sentence 2 and sentence 1. This observation is consistent with the claim that text embeddings encode more semantic meaning of the target texts and map semantically similar texts close to each other. 

2.3 Python API for Text Embedding 

The Python API for text embedding is provided in Python machine learning API for SAP HANA Cloud (hana-ml).  It is wrapped in a Python class called PALEmbeddings, with a fit_predict() method that accepts configurable parameters for generating embedding vectors from input texts. The configurable parameters are the same as those listed in the SQL API, except that they are in lower cases. However, there are also some major distinctions between the SQL API and the Python API, listed as follows: 

  • In SQL API for text embedding, all the four configurable parameters are provided in total for the procedure PAL_EMBEDDING; while in Python API, the parameter model_version and max_token_num are placed in initialization method of class PALEmbeddings, while the other three parameters are placed in its fit_predict() method. 
  •  In SQL API, the input data for embedding should be text pieces with IDs. In the Python API, the input data for the fit_predict() method of the PALEmbeddings class could be any data that contains the target text pieces and their corresponding IDs. The fit_predict() methods also provide two additional parameters, namely key and target to help users specify the ID column and target text column in the input data. Moreover, the result data generated by the Python API (i.e. the fit_predict() method of PALEmbedding) contains not only the text embeddings but also retains other non-target columns in the original input data. 

2.4. Code Example using the Python API 

To better illustrate the use of Python API for text embedding, we also show the python code for processing the texts in subsection 2.2. 

 

from hana_ml.text.pal_embeddings import PALEmbeddings 
embed_model = PALEmbeddings(model_version='20240715') 
#cc is assumed to ConnectionContext object representing a valid connection to SAP HANA Cloud instance 
#where text data (with IDs) is stored in the table SAMPLE_TEXT_TAB. 
text_data = cc.table('SAMPLE_TEXT_TAB') 
text_embed = embed_model.fit_predict(data=text_data,
                                     key='ID', 
                                     target='TEXT', 
                                     thread_number=20,                                                                    
                                     batch_size=25, 
                                     is_query=False)

 

To see the resulting text embeddings in the python interface, one can simply apply 

 

 

text_embed.collect()

 

 

which converts the SAP HANA DataFrame embed_res into a Pandas DataFrame in Python and then displays its content.  

Suppose we have a query sentence ‘What is the weather like today?’, and would like to retrieve a reasonable answer from the sentences in  SAMPLE_TEXT_TAB. Intuitively, we know that sentence 2 (‘The weather is awesome.’) and sentence 3 (‘Today is a sunny day.’) are both relevant while sentence 1 (‘The dog is awesome.’) is not relevant.  We can transform our query sentence into an embedding vector, and then compute the cosine similarity scores between the embedding vector of the query sentence and embedding vectors of the sentences in table SAMPLE_TEXT_TAB. Assuming that the query sentence is contained a table QUERY_TEXT_TAB structured as follows: 

 

ID 

QUERY 

1 

‘What is the weather like today?’ 

 

Continuing from the code for embedding the sentences in SAMPLE_TEXT_TAB, the code for query embedding and cosine similarity score computation can be illustrated as follows: 

 

 

query_data = cc.table('QUERY_TEXT_TAB') 
query_embed = embed_model.fit_predict(data=query_data, 
                                      key=’ID’,                                                                              
                                      target=’QUERY’,                                        
                                      is_query=True)#set as True since out the aim of input sentence is for query purpose. 
sim_scores = [] # for storing pair-wise similarity scores 
for i in [1,2,3]: 
    sql = “SELECT COSINE_SIMILARITY((SELECT VECTOR_COL FROM ({}) WHERE ID=1), (SELECT VECTOR FROM ({}) WHERE ID={})) AS COS_SIM_SCORE FROM DUMMY;”.format(query_embed.select_statement, text_embed.select_statement, i) 
    sim_scores.append(cc.sql(sql).collect()[“COS_SIM_SCORE”][0])

 

The resulting similarity scores are listed as follows: 

Sentence Pairs (Query ID, Text ID) 

(1, 1) 

(1, 2) 

(1, 3) 

Cosine Similarity Score 

0.342104 

0.617587 

0.700786 

As illustrated by the similarity scores, the query is much more related to Sentence 2 and Sentence 3 compared to Sentence 1 in SAMPLE_TEXT_TAB, which agrees with our intuition. 

3.Discussion on the Dimensionality of Text Embedding Vectors 

Embedding models are powerful tools for encoding non-structured texts by structured embedding vectors, greatly facilitating the use of text features in various applications and improving the results of traditional bag-of-words models. However, the benefits of using embedding vectors also come along with some extra costs. Besides the computational cost for generating text embeddings, the high-dimensional nature of embedding vectors also makes them storage demanding. Since many predictive analysis algorithms are sensitive to the dimensionality of the input data, they will require more computational and memory resources if embedding vectors get involved. 

To reduce the extra costs of using text embeddings, choosing a lower dimensional latent space is usually inappropriate for generic texts since it will result in significant loss of information in texts. However, since in most applications the text data is restricted to specific domains, it is possible to represent those texts by numerical vectors in a much lower dimensional latent space. One way to achieve this objective is to use universal text embedding models with high-dimensional latent space followed by application-dependent pre-processors for dimensionality reduction. To cater for this need, a new procedure called PAL_VECPCA is introduced in SAP HANA Cloud PAL, which can help to reduce the dimensionality of high-dimensional embedding vectors into lower-dimensional vectors via principal component analysis (PCA).  

 5. Summary 

In this blogpost we have introduced how to use text embedding services through the API provided by the SAP HANA Cloud Predictive Analysis Library (PAL).  Both SQL interface and Python interface are illustrated, with detailed parameter descriptions and code examples. We also discuss the dimensionality issue for text embedding vectors and provide the solution in SAP HANA Cloud PAL for alleviating this issue.

entry.title: New Text Analysis in SAP HANA Cloud Predictive Analysis Library (PAL)
entry.content:
1. Introduction 

Text Analysis aims at automatically extracting information from non-structured texts. For example, given a user comment, usually we want to know the focus of the comment context as well as the general sentiment it delivers. Text analysis is a traditional yet fundamental tool for processing texts, which has a wide range of applications in various real-life scenarios.  

However, text analysis has yet only been available with the SAP HANA Platform release for on-premises scenarios. Now, a new text analysis capability is introduced in SAP HANA Cloud with the predictive analysis library (PAL). In this blogpost we will provide a brief introduction to this new text analysis module.  

2. Text Analysis in SAP HANA Cloud PAL 

In SAP HANA Cloud PAL, given a piece of input text, Text Analysis performs the tasks of Named Entity Recognition (NER), Sentiment Analysis and Part-Of-Speech (POS) tagging, and is processed by a tiny-BERT NLP model, served within SAP HANA Cloud. It currently supports five languages: English, German, French, Spanish, and Portuguese. 

  • Named Entity Recognition (NER) is used for identifying entities within the target text. The scope of entities covers names of persons, organizations, locations, time expressions, etc. The primary goal of NER is to extract structured information from text, enabling machines to understand and categorize entities for various applications such as text summarization, building knowledge graphs, and question answering. 
  • Sentiment analysis is used to analyze the given piece of non-structured text to determine whether it expresses a sentiment that is either positive, negative, or neutral. It mainly applies to cases where it is important to explore the mood or mentality in context (e.g. comments and reviews). 
  • Part-of-Speech (POS) Tagging is to give each word in the given (unstructured) text its grammatical category like nouns, verbs, adjectives, and adverbs (the concepts are language dependent). It is essential for comprehending the syntactic structure of languages, and can benefit many NLP applications like text simplification, syntactic parsing, information retrieval, and machine translation. Moreover, POS tagging can also benefit the two text analysis tasks mentioned above, i.e. NER and sentiment analysis. 

The three tasks, namely NER, Sentiment Analysis and POS Tagging are of fundamental importance in natural language processing (NLP). Compared to the SAP HANA Platform text analysis implementation, the new text analysis module in SAP HANA Cloud gives a more comprehensive treatment for these tasks. In the new text analysis module for SAP HANA Cloud, sentiment analysis is performed with full hierarchy, which covers not only phrase/word-level sentiments but also sentence level sentiments as well as document-level sentiments.  

3. The SQL API 

To use the new text analysis functionalities in SAP HANA Cloud, users only need to call the PAL SQL procedure PAL_TEXT_ANALYSIS. Configurable parameters for this SQL procedure are listed as follows: 

  • THREAD_RATIO : Specifies the ratio of available threads to be used by this procedure. 
  • TIMEOUT :  Specifies the maximum amount of time (in seconds) the client will wait for a response from the server. 
  • BATCH_SIZE :  Specifies the number of text documents to be processed in a single batch, with 100 being the upper limit. 

Users can also specify the text analysis tasks (not necessarily all three) to perform for the input texts. However, tasks are not specified through parameters but rather in the input table. Basically, the input table of procedure PAL_TEXT_ANALYSIS should contain the four columns for representing text information listed as follows: 

  • Text/Document identifier (ID) 
  • Text content 
  • Text language 
  • Task 

Specifically, tasks are specified in string format, with ‘ner’ for NER, ‘pos’ for POS tagging, and ‘sentiment-phrase-score’ for sentiment analysis.  The tasks can be performed individually or in combination. Combination of tasks are represented by string compositions separated by commas, for example ‘ner, pos’ stands for two tasks, namely NER and POS tagging. 

3.1 First example 

In the following context we show the SQL script for analyzing the following simple text:  

“Alex used to be a successful software engineer. He had been passionate about programming.  

  Unfortunately, a serious issue at work took him out of his familiar territory.” 

 

 

 

-Firstly we create a table for the texts to be analyzed:  
CREATE COLUMN TABLE DATA_TAB( 
    "ID"  NVARCHAR(50), 
    "CONTENT" NVARCHAR(1000), 
    "LANGUAGE" NVARCHAR(1000), 
    "TASK" NVARCHAR(1000)
); 

INSERT INTO DATA_TAB VALUES('doc1', ' Alex used to be a successful software engineer. He had been passionate about programming. Unfortunately, a serious issue at work took him out of his familiar territory ', 'en', 'pos, ner, sentiment-phrase-score'); 
--then create the parameter table for parameter setting 
CREATE COLUMN TABLE PAL_PARAMETER_TAB( 
    "PARAM_NAME" NVARCHAR(256), 
    "INT_VALUE" INTEGER, 
    "DOUBLE_VALUE" DOUBLE, 
    "STRING_VALUE" NVARCHAR(1000) 
); 
INSERT INTO PAL_PARAMETER_TAB VALUES('TIMEOUT', 10, NULL, NULL); 
DO BEGIN 
lt_data = SELECT * FROM DATA_TAB; 
lt_param = SELECT * FROM PAL_PARAMETER_TAB; 
CALL _SYS_AFL.PAL_TEXT_ANALYSIS(:lt_data,:lt_param, lt_sentences, lt_pos,lt_ner,lt_doc_sentiment, lt_sentence_sentiment, lt_phrase_sentiment, lt_extra); 
SELECT * FROM :lt_sentences; 
SELECT * FROM :lt_pos; 
SELECT * FROM :lt_ner; 
SELECT * FROM :lt_doc_sentiment; 
SELECT * FROM :lt_sentence_sentiment; 
SELECT * FROM :lt_phrase_sentiment;  
END;

 

 

 

The respective output result sets can be inserted to dedicated tables, this step is omitted here for better readability. See the PAL_TEXT_ANALYSIS[link] documentation for an example. 

3.2 Result Analysis 

In this subsection we analyze the results of the previous example. To examine the result of a particular task, one only needs to select out the content from the corresponding table(s). 

3.2.1 Sentence Result 

The 1st output table of the procedure PAL_TEXT_ANALYSIS is the sentence result set, which contains the splitting result of target texts into sentences. For the example above, the sentence result is contained in the table variable :lt_sentences, the content is illustrated as follows: 

ID 

SENTENCE_ID 

CONTENT 

OFFSET 

GLOBAL_OFFSET 

1 

0 

Alex used to be a successful software engineer. 

0 

0 

1 

1 

He had been passionate about programming. 

0 

48 

1 

2 

Unfortunately, a serious issue at work took hi... 

0 

90 

It is seen from the result set that the procedure splits the input text into three sentences correctly as expected, with SENTENCE_ID representing the sentence order in the original text. 

3.2.2 POS Tagging Result 

POS tagging result is stored in 2nd   table of the procedure’s outputs. In the example above the table variable is :lt_pos, and its content is displayed as follows: 

ID 

SENTENCE_ID 

TOKEN 

ENTITY 

OFFSET 

GLOBAL_OFFSET 

1 

0 

Alex 

Prop 

0 

0 

1 

0 

used 

V-Past 

5 

5

0 

to 

Part-Inf 

10 

10

0 

be 

V-Inf-be 

13 

13 

... 

... 

... 

... 

... 

...

1 

He 

Pron 

0

had 

V-Past-have 

3

been 

V-PaPart-be

The sentiment analysis result splits into 3 tables that are for document-level sentiment, sentence-level sentiment, and phrase/word-level sentiment respectively. For the target text in the example, the document-level sentiment result is illustrated as follows:

Instead, it outputs two values, namely DOC_SENTIMENT_SCORE and DOC_SENTIMENT_MAGITUDE for users to judge the sentiment inclination of the input text/document. DOC_SENTIMENT_SCORE ranges from -1 to 1, and DOC_SENTIMENT_MAGNITUDE is always nonnegative. The sentiment inclination judging table for different combinations of the two values is as follows:
